coredata.dc:title: Bactericidal activity of Cannabis sativa phytochemicals from leaf extract and their derived Carbon Dots and Ag@Carbon Dots
coredata.dc:creator: Raina, Sheena
Thakur, Anupma
Sharma, Aditi
Pooja, D.
Minhas, Anu Priya
coredata.dc:description: The present was the first report on the synthesis of Carbon dots (CDs) using the acetone leaf extract of Cannabis sativa, an abundant weed plant as a sole carbon precursor. The obtained ~5 nm spherical CD particles (exhibiting 13% absolute quantum yield) were further employed to synthesize silver@CDs (Ag@CDs). CDs and Ag@CDs characterization revealed characteristic functional groups attributing to the aromatic, carboxylated and hydroxylated moieties. Ether and acetone leaf extracts of C. sativa exhibited strong antibacterial activity against dental microflora. Statistical analysis indicated 42 µg/mL as the significant MIC of Ag@CDs against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus (ρ < 0.0001). The results suggested weed abundance as an excellent natural carbon reserve and the biosynthesized Ag@CDs carry a great potential for the antibacterial application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cannabis
FTIR
Carbon dots
Structural
Nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: C. sativa leaf extract and microbial samples
Antibacterial and minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) assay
Optical and structural properties of CDs and Ag@CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption spectra of CDs and Ag@CDs, (b) emission spectra of CDs, (c) quantum yield measurement of CDs and (d) FT-IR spectra of CDs and Ag@CDs.
(a) HRTEM of CDs and (b) XRD spectra of CDs and Ag@CDs.

Antimicrobial assay of C. sativa (a) diethyl ether, (b) acetone leave extract (upper right panel), biosynthesized Ag@CDs (below panel) against (c) E. coli and (d) S. aureus and (e) dental microflora sample.

head.ce:title: Bactericidal activity of Cannabis sativa phytochemicals from leaf extract and their derived Carbon Dots and Ag@Carbon Dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, University Institute of Engineering and Technology, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University Institute of Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biotechnology, University Institute of Engineering and Technology, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR-Central Scientific Instruments Organisation, Chandigarh 160030, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CSIR-Central Scientific Instruments Organisation, Chandigarh 160030, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present was the first report on the synthesis of Carbon dots (CDs) using the acetone leaf extract of Cannabis sativa, an abundant weed plant as a sole carbon precursor. The obtained ~5 nm spherical CD particles (exhibiting 13% absolute quantum yield) were further employed to synthesize silver@CDs (Ag@CDs). CDs and Ag@CDs characterization revealed characteristic functional groups attributing to the aromatic, carboxylated and hydroxylated moieties. Ether and acetone leaf extracts of C. sativa exhibited strong antibacterial activity against dental microflora. Statistical analysis indicated 42 µg/mL as the significant MIC of Ag@CDs against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus (ρ < 0.0001). The results suggested weed abundance as an excellent natural carbon reserve and the biosynthesized Ag@CDs carry a great potential for the antibacterial application.

ce:section.ce:para: Present-day health challenge lie in discovering novel and natural antimicrobial agents. The antimicrobial activity of Cannabis sativa extracts has been lined to the presence of various phytocannabinoid [1]. Carbon dots (CD) are the eye-catcher due to their excitation dependent photoluminescence (PL) emission, high stability, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and facile surface chemistry as reviewed [2,3]. A facile, environment friendly and benign green technique to synthesize CDs utilizing low-cost source has emerged out as an alternate to complex synthesis procedure for commercial viability [4,5,6]. Functional groups present on biological metabolites can reduce metal ion and stabilize the synthesized nanoparticles with additional advantage of enhanced biological activities. Furthermore, functional groups on the surface of biosynthesized CDs have been used to synthesize several metallic nanoparticles thereby reducing human exposure to harmful reducing and capping chemicals [7]. Recently, Singh et al (2018) reported the synthesis and antimicrobial activity of silver and gold nanoparticles using Cannabis stem extract [8].
The present work is the first report on synthesis, characterization and antimicrobial activity of biosynthesized CDs and Ag@CDs using acetone leaf extract of C. sativa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: C. sativa leaf extract and microbial samples
ce:section.ce:para: Chemicals and solvents were procured from Fisher Scientific and Sisco Research Laboratories. Dental samples were collected randomly from students of UIET, Panjab University and analyzed using Gram staining. Escherichia coli (E. coli) DH10β and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) strains were available in our laboratory. C. sativa leaves were collected from different localities of Chandigarh and verified from Botany, Panjab Uinversity, Chandigarh. Fine dry leaves powder was extracted with diethyl ether (1:5) for 72 h, clarified by filtration and lyophilized. Solid residual filtrate was subsequently extracted with acetone following same procedure.

CDs were synthesized using a one-step pyrolysis method using acetone extract prepared from C. sativa leaf as portrayed in Supplementary Scheme 1 and as described in Supplementary file section 1.1 [9]. Briefly, for Ag@CDs synthesis, mixture of CDs and 0.01 M AgNO3 solutions (3:1) (pH adjusted with ammonia) was stirred in dark for 1 h at room temperature, dialyzed overnight and stored in the refrigerator. Hydrophilic functionalities present on CDs surface promote the reduction of AgNO3 and stabilization of Ag nanoparticles [7].

(UV)–visible (Shimadzu Analytical India Pvt. Ltd) in 200–800 nm wavelength with 1 nm resolution and photoluminescence spectrophotometer (FS5 Edinburg Instruments) were performed for analyzing optical and emission properties of synthesized CDs and Ag@CDs. High-resolution TEM and X-ray diffraction were employed for their structural analysis while surface functionalities were confirmed with Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial and minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) assay
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity was assessed using disk diffusion and microplate assay as previously described [10,11]. MIC defined as the lowest concentration that inhibits bacterial growth.
The mean, standard deviation and analysis of variance were performed by One-way ANOVA while multiple comparisons was done using Tukey Post Hoc test (SPSS version 20.0).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and structural properties of CDs and Ag@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 (a) revealed the absorption characteristics recorded for CDs and Ag@CDs, depicting the presence of broad absorption peak at ~330 nm and in UV region. The peaks attributed to the n → π* and π → π* molecular transitions associated with CO groups and CC architecture of CDs [12]. The absorption spectrum of Ag@CDs exhibiting an additional peak at 410 nm assigned to surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band for Ag nanoparticles, implies the synthesis of Ag@CDs from CDs as reducing and stabilizing agent [13].
Fig. 1(b) portrayed the excitation-dependent emission behavior of synthesized CDs, which could be due to the presence of uniform sp2 clusters [14].
Fig. 1(c) represented ~13% of absolute quantum yield (QY) of the as-synthesized CDs measured using integrated sphere-quantum yield assembly.

Fig. 1(d) represented FT-IR spectrum of CDs and Ag@CDs which reveals the characteristic functional groups of CC stretch at ~1500 cm−1 attributing to aromatic carbonaceous nature, CO stretch at ~1680 cm−1 of carboxylated moieties and –OH stretch at ~3400 cm−1 of hydroxylated groups which in scripts their high hydrophilic nature [15].

Fig. 2 (a) depicted the morphological and structural characteristics of synthesized CDs probed by high-resolution transmission microscope (HR-TEM). The micrograph revealed that the CDs have spherical morphology with an average diameter of ~5 nm.

Fig. 2(b) showed the XRD spectra of CDs and Ag@CDs, where CDs show amorphous nature. While in Ag@CDs, the significant peaks at 38.2°, 44.4°, 57.6°, 73.2° and 77.5° can be assigned to the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0), (3 1 1) and (2 2 2) planes of Ag nanostructures (JCPDS Card Number 89-3697). The peak at ~30° is coming from the Si (1 1 1) substrate [16,17].

Fig. 3 (upper right panel) showed inhibition zone of ~12 mm diameter for C. sativa leaf extract against dental microflora. MIC value of (a) 5.0 and (b) 2.5 mg/ml were reported for diethyl ether and acetone extract respectively.

Fig. 3c and d (lower panel) showed significant difference in antibacterial activity (as mean A600, +SD bars) between different Ag@CDs concentrations used against these microorganisms (ρ < 0.0001). Synthesized Ag@CDs exhibited ~90% killing of E. coli as well as S. aureus at MIC of 42 µg/mL. However, none of Ag@CDs concentration could be reported as significant MIC (maximum killing at 25 µg/mL concentration) against dental micro flora due to heterogeneous nature of the culture and their varying susceptibility to Ag@CDs.

Limited studies have been reported on the antibacterial activity of plant-based CDs in recent past [18,19]. A high concentration (upto 50 mg/ml) of biosynthesized CDs in this study is not inhibitory to bacteria, suggesting detailed structural and functional investigations of more bio-based CDs Supplementary Fig. 2.

Plants are the rich sources of bioactive molecules. The abundance and potency of weed plants can be exploited for the benefit of mankind with promising therapeutic potential against virulent microbes. Here we reported the simple and eco-friendly synthesis of CDs and Ag@CDs with excellent PL properties using acetone leave extract of C. sativa. C. sativa leaf extracts and synthesized Ag@CDs showed dose sensitive antimicrobial property against E. coli and S. aureus. Being human as the end user, toxicity studies of these nanoparticles needs to be carried out. These findings also emphasized on the huge scope of natural carbon precursor from abundant weed plants in the diverse field of nanotechnology, its therapeutic and medical applications.
This research work is supported by TEQIP, UIET.

Sheena Raina: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Anupma Thakur: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Aditi Sharma: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Pooja D.: Conceptualization, Validation, Writing - original draft. Anu Priya Minhas: Conceptualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Special thanks to Dr. Sanjeev Puri, UIET, for supporting the work. Director, CSIO is acknowledged for his support to carry out this study in collaboration.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2019.127122.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibacterial cannabinoids from Cannabis sativa: a structure−activity study
